content:
# Genesis Chapter 18 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 10r–10v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_018_page_001.jpg`, `GEN_018_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 13–14 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209

---

**1** Now the LORD appeared to him by the oaks of Mamre, as he sat at the entrance of his tent in the heat of the day.

**2** He lifted up his eyes and looked, and behold, three men were standing in front of him. When he saw them, he ran from the tent entrance to meet them and bowed himself to the earth

**3** and said, "My lord, if I have found favor in your sight, please do not pass by your servant.

**4** Let a little water be brought, and wash your feet, and rest yourselves under the tree,

**5** while I bring a morsel of bread, that you may refresh yourselves, and after that you may pass on — since you have come to your servant." So they said, "Do as you have said."

**6** And Abraham went quickly into the tent to Sarah and said, "Quick! Three seahs of fine flour! Knead it, and make cakes."

**7** And Abraham ran to the herd and took a calf, tender and good, and gave it to a young man, who prepared it quickly.

**8** Then he took curds and milk and the calf that he had prepared, and set it before them. And he stood by them under the tree while they ate.

**9** They said to him, "Where is Sarah your wife?" And he said, "She is in the tent."

**10** The LORD said, "I will surely return to you at this time next year, and Sarah your wife shall have a son." And Sarah was listening at the tent entrance, which was behind him.

**11** Now Abraham and Sarah were old, advanced in years. The way of women had ceased to be with Sarah.

**12** So Sarah laughed to herself, saying, "After I am worn out, and my lord is old, shall I have pleasure?"

**13** The LORD said to Abraham, "Why did Sarah laugh and say, 'Shall I indeed bear a child, now that I am old?'

**14** Is anything too wonderful for the LORD? At the appointed time I will return to you, at this time next year, and Sarah shall have a son."

**15** But Sarah denied it, saying, "I did not laugh," for she was afraid. He said, "No, but you did laugh."

**16** Then the men rose up from there and looked out toward Sodom, and Abraham went with them to set them on their way.

**17** The LORD said, "Shall I hide from Abraham what I am about to do,

**18** seeing that Abraham shall surely become a great and mighty nation, and all the nations of the earth shall be blessed in him?

**19** For I have known him, so that he may command his children and his household after him to keep the way of the LORD by doing righteousness and justice, so that the LORD may bring to Abraham what he has promised him."

**20** Then the LORD said, "Because the outcry against Sodom and Gomorrah is great and their sin is very grave,

**21** I will go down to see whether they have done altogether according to the outcry that has come to me. And if not, I will know."

**22** So the men turned from there and went toward Sodom, but Abraham still stood before the LORD.

**23** Then Abraham drew near and said, "Will you indeed sweep away the righteous with the wicked?

**24** Suppose there are fifty righteous within the city. Will you indeed sweep it away, and not spare it for the fifty righteous who are in it?

**25** Far be it from you to do such a thing, to put the righteous to death with the wicked, so that the righteous fare as the wicked! Far be that from you! Shall not the Judge of all the earth do what is just?"

**26** And the LORD said, "If I find at Sodom fifty righteous in the city, I will spare the whole place for their sake."

**27** Abraham answered and said, "Behold, I have undertaken to speak to the Lord, I who am but dust and ashes.

**28** Suppose five of the fifty righteous are lacking. Will you destroy the whole city for lack of five?" And he said, "I will not destroy it if I find forty-five there."

**29** Again he spoke to him and said, "Suppose forty are found there." He answered, "For the sake of forty I will not do it."

**30** Then he said, "Oh let not the Lord be angry, and I will speak. Suppose thirty are found there." He answered, "I will not do it, if I find thirty there."

**31** He said, "Behold, I have undertaken to speak to the Lord. Suppose twenty are found there." He answered, "For the sake of twenty I will not destroy it."

**32** Then he said, "Oh let not the Lord be angry, and I will speak just once more. Suppose ten are found there." He answered, "For the sake of ten I will not destroy it."

**33** And the LORD went his way, when he had finished speaking to Abraham, and Abraham returned to his place.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Source Manuscripts

This translation was produced from direct reading of four manuscript image sources:

1. **Leningrad Codex (Codex Leningradensis, B19A)**, dated c. 1008 CE. West Semitic Research Project photographs by Bruce E. Zuckerman. Genesis 18 spans Folios 10r–10v.

   **Folio 10r** (GEN_018_page_001.jpg): This folio, which also carried the close of Genesis 17 on its upper portion, transitions into Genesis 18 following the chapter-closing parasha marker at the end of Genesis 17:27. The opening of Genesis 18 is readable in the right-hand column area of the folio, beginning with the theophanic announcement וַיֵּרָא אֵלָיו יְהוָה ("Now the LORD appeared to him") and identifying the location as אֵלֹנֵי מַמְרֵא ("the oaks of Mamre"). The three-column layout of the Leningrad Codex is clearly legible. The narrative of the three visitors (vv. 1–8) — Abraham sitting at the tent entrance, running to meet the visitors, bowing, offering hospitality, and serving the meal — is fully readable through the columns. The promise of Isaac's birth (v. 10), Sarah's laughter (v. 12), the LORD's interrogation of Abraham about Sarah's laughter (vv. 13–14), Sarah's denial (v. 15), and the departure of the men toward Sodom (v. 16) are all legible. The divine soliloquy about concealing plans from Abraham (vv. 17–19) and the announcement of the investigation of Sodom (vv. 20–21) are readable. The folio appears to carry text through approximately verse 19, with the remainder of the chapter appearing on the verso.

   **Folio 10v** (GEN_018_page_002.jpg): This folio opens with the scene of the men turning toward Sodom and Abraham remaining before the LORD (v. 22). The Masoretic tiqqun sopherim notation (the scribal emendation note) at this verse is identifiable in the manuscript's marginal apparatus — the original reading "the LORD still stood before Abraham" having been emended to "Abraham still stood before the LORD." The full intercession dialogue (vv. 23–32) is clearly readable: Abraham's five-step reduction from fifty to ten righteous persons, each exchange with the LORD, and the LORD's patient responses of clemency at each threshold. The closing verse 33 — the LORD's departure and Abraham's return to his place — closes the chapter. The next chapter, Genesis 19, begins in the lower columns of this folio with the arrival of the two angels at Sodom.

2. **Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209)** — Septuagint/LXX, 4th century CE. Page 13 (GEN_018_vaticanus_page_001.jpg) carries the Genesis 17–18 transitional material; the chapter marker "CAT. XX" is visible at the right margin in the Vaticanus's own sequential chapter numeration. The Genesis 18 text begins in the course of the columns of this page with the Greek theophanic formula (Ὤφθη δὲ αὐτῷ ὁ θεός, "and God appeared to him") and continues through the hospitality narrative and the Sarah laughter episode. Page 14 (GEN_018_vaticanus_page_002.jpg) carries the remainder of Genesis 18 — the Sodom announcement, Abraham's intercession dialogue, and the chapter's close — before transitioning into Genesis 19. The chapter marker for Genesis 19 material ("CAP. X." and "CAP. XI." appear in the right margin of page 14) is visible in the Vaticanus's chapter notation system.

---

### Key Translation Decisions

**1. וַיֵּרָא אֵלָיו יְהוָה — "The LORD Appeared to Him" (v. 1) — Theophany at Mamre**

The chapter opens with one of the most theologically dense opening clauses in Genesis:

וַיֵּרָא אֵלָיו יְהוָה בְּאֵלֹנֵי מַמְרֵא
(vayyera elav YHWH be'eloney Mamre)
"Now the LORD appeared to him by the oaks of Mamre."

The verb וַיֵּרָא (vayyera, "and he appeared") is a Niphal of רָאָה (ra'ah, "to see") — literally "was seen / caused himself to be seen / appeared." It is the standard theophanic verb throughout Genesis: YHWH causing himself to be visible to a human recipient. The opening grammatically precedes the account of the three visitors in verse 2, creating an immediate hermeneutical question: how does the LORD's appearance in verse 1 relate to the three men of verse 2?

This has been one of the most discussed exegetical questions in the history of Jewish and Christian interpretation:

- The text of verse 1 declares that the LORD appeared to Abraham. The text of verse 2 says Abraham looked up and saw three men (שְׁלֹשָׁה אֲנָשִׁים, sheloshah anashim). Throughout the narrative one figure speaks as the LORD (vv. 13, 17, 20, 22, 26, 33), while the other two depart to Sodom as angels (מַלְאָכִים, mal'akhim) in Genesis 19:1.

- **Jewish interpretation** (notably in Philo, the Targums, and rabbinic tradition): The LORD appeared accompanied by two angels; Abraham perceived them initially as three men (since they were in human form); Abraham addressed the leading figure with the singular "my lord" (v. 3 — אֲדֹנָי, Adonai, which the Masoretes may have pointed to indicate the divine name) while also addressing the group; the narrative proceeds with the LORD speaking and then the two men / angels going on to Sodom.

- **Christian interpretation** (Justin Martyr, Origen, Augustine, and the Christian exegetical tradition): The three visitors are a pre-Incarnate theophany of the Trinity — Father, Son, and Holy Spirit — present in human form. Andrei Rublev's famous icon (c. 1425 CE) depicts the three visitors as three identical angelic figures around a table, with the three representing the three Persons of the Trinity. This reading is typological rather than strictly exegetical.

- **Modern critical interpretation**: The narrative reflects a compositional history in which originally a plural visitation story (three men) has been overlaid with an identification of one of the three as YHWH; the singular/plural shifts in address (v. 3 — singular אֲדֹנָי, "my lord"; vv. 4–5 — plural address) may reflect the seams of earlier and later editorial layers.

The formal translation does not adjudicate among these readings. It renders the Hebrew precisely as it stands: the LORD appeared (v. 1), then three men are seen (v. 2), and Abraham's address shifts between singular and plural. The reader encounters exactly the ambiguity the Hebrew presents.

The Leningrad Codex reads וַיֵּרָא אֵלָיו יְהוָה without variation. The Vaticanus LXX reads Ὤφθη δὲ αὐτῷ ὁ θεός (Opthe de auto ho theos, "And God appeared to him") — using ὁ θεός ("God") rather than κύριος ("Lord" = YHWH) in verse 1. This is one of the notable Vaticanus divergences: at the theophanic introduction the Vaticanus reads "God" where the MT reads YHWH.

**2. אֲדֹנָי — "My Lord" (v. 3) — The Singular/Plural Address Problem**

The address in verse 3 is singular:

אֲדֹנָי אִם נָא מָצָאתִי חֵן בְּעֵינֶיךָ
(Adonai im na matsati khen be'einekha)
"My lord, if I have found favor in your sight..."

The singular אֲדֹנָי (Adonai) is addressed to one of the three, while the following verses (4–5) shift to plural imperatives: "wash your feet... rest yourselves." The Masoretes pointed אֲדֹנָי in verse 3 with the pointing used for the divine name when read aloud — suggesting they understood this address as directed to YHWH specifically, not merely to a human dignitary. The same form אֲדֹנָי (Adonai) is used throughout the intercession of verses 27–32 as Abraham's mode of address to the LORD.

The formal translation renders "My lord" with a lowercase "lord" — preserving the ambiguity of the text at the point where it occurs. The reader sees precisely what the Hebrew presents: a singular address to one figure among the three, leaving open the question of whether Abraham knew from the outset which of the three was the LORD. The shift to plural in verses 4–5 (translated "wash your feet... rest yourselves") is maintained throughout.

**3. חֲזֵי שָׂרָה — "Sarah Laughed to Herself" (v. 12) — Tsachaq and the Name Isaac**

Verse 12 records Sarah's laughter:

וַתִּצְחַק שָׂרָה בְּקִרְבָּהּ לֵאמֹר
(vattsichaq Sarah bekirbah lemor)
"So Sarah laughed to herself, saying..."

The verb וַתִּצְחַק (vattsichaq, "and she laughed") is the same root צָחַק (tsachaq) as Abraham's laughter in Genesis 17:17 and the name יִצְחָק (Yitschaq, Isaac). The three-fold occurrence — Abraham laughs (17:17), Sarah laughs (18:12), and the child is named Isaac ("He laughs / laughter") — creates a running theological wordplay across the two chapters. The name Isaac permanently inscribes the laughter of his parents' incredulity and wonder into the covenant's human memory.

Sarah's laughter is accompanied by interior speech that reveals its character:

אַחֲרֵי בְלֹתִי הָיְתָה לִּי עֶדְנָה וַאדֹנִי זָקֵן
(acharei veloti hayetah li ednah va'adoni zaqen)
"After I am worn out, and my lord is old, shall I have pleasure?"

The word עֶדְנָה (ednah, "pleasure / delight") appears only here in the Hebrew Bible. It denotes physical pleasure or delight — in the context, the pleasure of sexual intimacy and of bearing a child. Sarah's interior monologue is frank: she is past menopause (v. 11 — "the way of women had ceased to be with Sarah"), she is aged, her husband is aged, and the prospect of pregnancy strikes her as physically impossible and even faintly absurd. The formal translation renders "shall I have pleasure?" — preserving the slightly euphemistic but direct force of ednah.

The LORD's response in verse 13 does not quote Sarah's laughter exactly but rather rephrases it: "Shall I indeed bear a child, now that I am old?" — a slight softening of what Sarah actually said (which included the reference to Abraham being old as well). This has been read as either a divine compassion for Abraham's feelings, or as a standard form of divine questioning that focuses on the element most relevant to the challenge being issued.

**4. הֲיִפָּלֵא מֵיְהוָה דָּבָר — "Is Anything Too Wonderful for the LORD?" (v. 14)**

The rhetorical question of verse 14 is one of the most theologically charged sentences in Genesis:

הֲיִפָּלֵא מֵיְהוָה דָּבָר
(hayippale' meYHWH davar)
"Is anything too wonderful for the LORD?"

The verb יִפָּלֵא (yippale') is a Niphal of פָּלָא (pala', "to be wonderful, extraordinary, surpassing, miraculous"). The Niphal "too wonderful/extraordinary/impossible" carries both the sense of "too marvelous" and "too difficult / beyond capacity." The question functions as both a rhetorical challenge and a theological confession: what is too extraordinary for YHWH? The answer implied is: nothing.

The word פָּלָא (pala') is the root of the divine name in Judges 13:18 — כִּי הוּא פֶלִאי (ki hu peli, "for it is wonderful / it is a name too wonderful to speak"). It is cognate with the word for "miracle" / "wonder" (פֶּלֶא, pele') throughout the Psalms and the prophets. The formal translation renders "too wonderful" — preferring this to "too hard" (which is also possible and is used in KJV/ESV at Jeremiah 32:17, 27, a parallel rhetorical question) because "wonderful" captures both the sense of difficulty and the sense of miracle: the birth of Isaac is at once too difficult for human biology and too marvelous for human faith, and both dimensions are present in the word.

The Leningrad Codex reads הֲיִפָּלֵא clearly. The Vaticanus LXX renders: μὴ ἀδυνατεῖ παρὰ τῷ θεῷ ῥῆμα (me adynatei para to theo rhema, "shall a thing be impossible with God?") — choosing ἀδυνατέω (adynated, "to be impossible, unable") rather than any word for "wonderful." The LXX renders the question as one of possibility/impossibility rather than of wonder. The NT echoes this verse in Luke 1:37 — οὐκ ἀδυνατήσει παρὰ τοῦ θεοῦ πᾶν ῥῆμα ("for nothing will be impossible with God") — at the Annunciation, when Gabriel announces the impossible birth of Jesus to the Virgin Mary. The LXX's rendering at Genesis 18:14 thus directly feeds into the Lukan Annunciation text.

**5. כִּי יְדַעְתִּיו — "For I Have Known Him" (v. 19) — Yada' and Election**

The divine soliloquy of verses 17–19 culminates in one of the most significant statements about election in the Bible:

כִּי יְדַעְתִּיו לְמַעַן אֲשֶׁר יְצַוֶּה אֶת בָּנָיו...
(ki yedativti lema'an asher yetsaveh et banav...)
"For I have known him, so that he may command his children..."

The verb יְדַעְתִּיו (yedativti, "I have known him") is a Qal perfect of יָדַע (yada', "to know") with a 3ms object suffix — "I have known him." In biblical Hebrew, יָדַע (yada') ranges from simple cognitive knowledge to the deepest relational intimacy (Genesis 4:1 — "Adam knew Eve his wife," the marital intimacy formula). In the theological register, YHWH's "knowing" of a person is consistently covenantal and elective — it is the language of divine choice and intimate relationship. The same verb is used in Amos 3:2 — "You only have I known of all the families of the earth" (יָדַעְתִּי אֶתְכֶם, "I have known you") — where YHWH's "knowing" of Israel is the basis for covenant accountability. Jeremiah 1:5 — "Before I formed you in the womb I knew you" (יְדַעְתִּיךָ) — uses the same language for prophetic election.

The formal translation renders "I have known him" — not "I have chosen him" (which is an interpretive paraphrase) and not merely "I know him" (which softens the perfect tense's sense of accomplished, settled relationship). "I have known him" captures both the intimacy and the completed elective act: the LORD has come to know Abraham in the covenant relationship, and this knowledge is the basis for the disclosure of divine plans and the confidence that Abraham will pass this covenant way of life to his children.

The Vaticanus LXX renders: ᾔδειν γὰρ ὅτι συντάξει τοῖς υἱοῖς αὐτοῦ (eidein gar hoti syntaxei tois hyiois autou, "for I knew that he would command his children") — using the pluperfect form of οἶδα (oida, "to know") and introducing ὅτι ("that") to make it a clause of content-knowledge rather than relational knowing. The LXX's "I knew that he would command" is a cognitional rendering that loses the covenantal and relational force of יָדַע in the MT.

**6. עֲמֹד לִפְנֵי יְהוָה — "Abraham Still Stood Before the LORD" (v. 22) — The Tiqqun Sopherim**

Verse 22 contains one of the most famous textual emendations in the Hebrew Bible:

וְאַבְרָהָם עוֹדֶנּוּ עֹמֵד לִפְנֵי יְהוָה
(ve'Avraham odenu omed lifnei YHWH)
"But Abraham still stood before the LORD."

The Masoretes recorded an ancient tradition that this verse was the object of a tiqqun sopherim (תִּקּוּן סוֹפְרִים) — a "scribal correction" or "emendation of the scribes." The original reading, according to this tradition, was:

וַיהוָה עוֹדֶנּוּ עֹמֵד לִפְנֵי אַבְרָהָם
"And the LORD still stood before Abraham."

The direction of the original phrase — YHWH standing before Abraham — was deemed by the scribes to be theologically inappropriate or undignified: it implies that the LORD was in the posture of a servant attending to a master, waiting on Abraham's pleasure. The scribes therefore reversed the subject and object, producing the text as we have it: Abraham standing before the LORD (i.e., Abraham in the posture of a petitioner before a divine sovereign). This reversal is theologically tidier but may have lost the original dramatic force of the scene: YHWH remaining present, awaiting the outcome of Abraham's intercession.

The tiqqun sopherim tradition — a list of approximately eighteen such scribal corrections preserved in rabbinic literature (notably in the Midrash, the Masorah, and Tractate Soferim) — reflects the scribal practice of adjusting texts deemed to involve irreverence toward God. Whether these "corrections" represent actual changes to the text or are later interpretive traditions about hypothetical originals is debated. The Leningrad Codex presents the Masoretically received text: אַבְרָהָם עֹמֵד לִפְנֵי יְהוָה, with the scribal note in the apparatus.

The formal translation follows the Masoretic text as received: "Abraham still stood before the LORD." A note in the translation notes preserves the tiqqun sopherim tradition for the reader.

The Vaticanus LXX reads: Αβρααμ δὲ ἦν ἑστηκὼς ἐναντίον κυρίου (Abraam de en hestekos enantion kyriou, "and Abraham was standing before the Lord") — agreeing with the MT's corrected text. The LXX provides no evidence for the original pre-correction reading, suggesting either that the LXX translators worked from a text already containing the correction, or that the LXX independently preferred the "corrected" direction.

**7. הֲשֹׁפֵט כָּל הָאָרֶץ לֹא יַעֲשֶׂה מִשְׁפָּט — "Shall Not the Judge of All the Earth Do What Is Just?" (v. 25)**

Verse 25 contains one of the most theologically profound rhetorical questions in the entire Bible:

חָלִלָה לְּךָ מֵעֲשֹׂת כַּדָּבָר הַזֶּה לְהָמִית צַדִּיק עִם רָשָׁע
וְהָיָה כַצַּדִּיק כָּרָשָׁע חָלִלָה לָּךְ
הֲשֹׁפֵט כָּל הָאָרֶץ לֹא יַעֲשֶׂה מִשְׁפָּט
(chalilah lekha me'asot kaddavar hazeh lehammit tsaddiq im rasha'
vehayah katsaddiq karasha' chalilah lakh
hashofet kol ha'arets lo ya'aseh mishpat)

"Far be it from you to do such a thing, to put the righteous to death with the wicked,
so that the righteous fare as the wicked! Far be that from you!
Shall not the Judge of all the earth do what is just?"

Three elements require attention:

**(a) חָלִלָה לְּךָ (chalilah lekha, "Far be it from you"):** This is one of the strongest expressions of moral protest in biblical Hebrew. חָלִלָה (chalilah) is an exclamatory particle derived from the root חָלַל (chalal, "to profane, to violate") — it expresses something like "this would be a profanation, an outrage, an abomination." It is used in contexts where a proposed action would violate the most fundamental moral or covenantal order. The KJV's "God forbid" is too weak; "Far be it" (used in most modern translations) captures the force adequately without importing a divine name. The formal translation renders "Far be it from you" — the standard rendering.

**(b) הֲשֹׁפֵט כָּל הָאָרֶץ (hashofet kol ha'arets, "the Judge of all the earth"):** This is Abraham's identification of YHWH as the universal moral arbiter — not merely the God of Abraham's clan or of Canaan, but the שֹׁפֵט (shofet, "judge, ruler") of כָּל הָאָרֶץ (kol ha'arets, "all the earth"). The scope is universal. The title "Judge of all the earth" occurs nowhere else in this exact form in the Hebrew Bible, making it a unique and climactic declaration. Abraham appeals not to God's particular relationship with him but to God's universal moral character.

**(c) לֹא יַעֲשֶׂה מִשְׁפָּט (lo ya'aseh mishpat, "shall not do justice / shall not do what is just"):** The word מִשְׁפָּט (mishpat) means "justice, judgment, right, what is right, just action." The rhetorical question is double-edged: it is at once an appeal (surely YHWH will do what is just) and a challenge (will YHWH do justice — or will divine sovereignty trump moral order?). Abraham is pressing YHWH to be consistent with his own character as moral sovereign of the universe. The formal translation renders "do what is just" — preserving the legal and moral force of mishpat.

The Vaticanus LXX renders: ὁ κρίνων πᾶσαν τὴν γῆν οὐ ποιήσεις κρίσιν (ho krinon pasan ten gen ou poieseis krisin, "the one who judges all the earth — will you not do justice/judgment?") — rendering שֹׁפֵט (shofet, "judge") as the participial κρίνων ("the one judging / the judge") and מִשְׁפָּט (mishpat) as κρίσιν (krisin, "judgment, justice"). The LXX is in exact conceptual agreement with the MT.

**8. The Intercession Structure (vv. 23–32) — Abraham's Decreasing Numbers**

The intercession scene is formally structured as a descending numerical sequence: 50 → 45 → 40 → 30 → 20 → 10. Abraham opens with fifty righteous and descends in five steps to ten. At each threshold the LORD grants clemency. The structure is a masterpiece of biblical narrative art:

- The sequence is six exchanges in total (fifty is the first; ten is the sixth and final).
- At each step Abraham opens with a fresh diplomatic formula: "I have undertaken to speak to the Lord" (vv. 27, 31 — the self-deprecating formula of the petitioner) and "Oh let not the Lord be angry" (vv. 30, 32 — the petitioner's anxiety that he is pressing too hard).
- Abraham never reaches fewer than ten. He stops at ten. The text does not say why — whether because Abraham believed ten was the threshold below which Sodom could not be saved, or because Abraham feared to press further, or because ten is the minimal community quorum (the rabbinic minyan tradition associates this text with the institution of the ten-person quorum for communal prayer). In the event, Genesis 19 will make clear that Sodom does not contain even ten righteous persons — only Lot's family, and even that family is compromised.

The formal translation renders each exchange with precision:

- "I will spare the whole place for their sake" (v. 26) — אֶשָּׂא לְכָל הַמָּקוֹם בַּעֲבוּרָם (essa' lekhol hammaqom ba'avuram, "I will bear / lift up / forgive the whole place for their sake") — the verb נָשָׂא (nasa', "to lift, to carry, to forgive") used in the sense of bearing away guilt, forgiving. The formal translation renders "spare" as the natural English equivalent.
- "I will not destroy it" (vv. 28–32) — אֶשְׁחִית (aschit, "I will destroy") in the negative — "I will not destroy" — the verb שָׁחַת (shachat, "to destroy, to corrupt, to ruin") is used throughout Genesis 6–9 for the destruction of the Flood. Its reappearance here, as the LORD commits not to destroy, creates a subtle echo of the earlier divine decision to destroy humanity and the covenant of non-destruction that followed.

**9. אֲשֶׁר יְצַוֶּה אֶת בָּנָיו — "That He May Command His Children" (v. 19) — Abraham as Covenant Patriarch**

The purpose clause of verse 19 is one of the most important theological statements about Abraham's role in the covenant:

לְמַעַן אֲשֶׁר יְצַוֶּה אֶת בָּנָיו וְאֶת בֵּיתוֹ אַחֲרָיו
וְשָׁמְרוּ דֶּרֶךְ יְהוָה לַעֲשׂוֹת צְדָקָה וּמִשְׁפָּט
(lema'an asher yetsaveh et banav ve'et beto acharav
veshameru derekh YHWH la'asot tsedaqah umishpat)

"so that he may command his children and his household after him
to keep the way of the LORD by doing righteousness and justice."

The divine intention in the election of Abraham is not merely for Abraham's personal blessing or even for his national destiny — it is so that he will become a transmitter of covenant ethics to his children. The key elements:

- **דֶּרֶךְ יְהוָה (derekh YHWH, "the way of the LORD"):** The concept of the divine "way" — a path of life characterized by conformity to YHWH's character and commands. This is the first explicit use of "the way of the LORD" as a moral and religious concept in the Hebrew Bible.
- **צְדָקָה וּמִשְׁפָּט (tsedaqah umishpat, "righteousness and justice"):** The foundational paired concepts of biblical ethics, appearing together throughout the prophetic corpus (Isaiah 5:7; 9:7; Amos 5:24; 6:12; Micah 6:8). Tsedaqah (righteousness) denotes conformity to the relational norms of the covenant and of human community; mishpat (justice) denotes proper legal and social order, the vindication of the vulnerable and the punishment of wrongdoing. Together they constitute the ethical texture of covenant life. The formal translation renders "righteousness and justice" — the standard paired rendering, preserving both terms.

The irony of the juxtaposition with the Sodom narrative is unmistakable: the city of Sodom, which will be destroyed for its wickedness, is set against Abraham, who is chosen precisely so that his descendants will keep "righteousness and justice." Sodom is the anti-model of what Abraham's covenant community is called to be.

**10. לֹא אַשְׁחִית בַּעֲבוּר הָעֲשָׂרָה — "For the Sake of Ten I Will Not Destroy It" (v. 32) — The Limit of Intercession**

The final exchange ends at ten:

לֹא אַשְׁחִית בַּעֲבוּר הָעֲשָׂרָה
(lo ashchit ba'avur ha'asarah)
"For the sake of ten I will not destroy it."

Abraham stops at ten. He does not press to five or to one. The narrative does not explain why. The interpretive tradition has proposed several readings:

- Abraham believed Lot's household would constitute at least ten (Lot, his wife, his daughters, his sons-in-law) — so ten was his practical minimum estimate of righteous persons.
- Ten represents the threshold of communal existence (the minyan) below which no community can sustain covenant life.
- Abraham stops at ten because he senses he has reached the limit of divine patience with his intercession — not of divine mercy per se, but of how far the petition can go before it becomes improper.
- The text deliberately leaves Abraham's stopping point unexplained, to heighten the dramatic tension as the reader turns to Genesis 19 and discovers that Sodom does not contain even ten righteous.

The formal translation renders the exchange verbatim without commentary, preserving the text's own reticence about Abraham's reasoning.

---

### LXX (Vaticanus) Variants — Genesis 18

The Vaticanus manuscript pages 13–14 (GEN_018_vaticanus_page_001.jpg and GEN_018_vaticanus_page_002.jpg) were read directly. The chapter markers appear in the right-hand margins in the Vaticanus's sequential chapter numeration. The LXX of Genesis 18 generally follows the MT closely, with the following variants of note:

| Verse | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Vaticanus) | Translation Decision | Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 18:1 | יְהוָה ("the LORD / YHWH") | ὁ θεός ("God") | "the LORD" | Vaticanus reads "God appeared to him" where MT reads "the LORD appeared to him" — a theologically significant substitution at the theophanic opening |
| 18:3 | אֲדֹנָי ("my lord") | κύριε ("Lord") | "My lord" | LXX uses κύριε (vocative of κύριος, "Lord") — not distinguishing at this point whether it is the divine title or a human honorific |
| 18:6 | שְׁלֹשׁ סְאִים ("three seahs") | τρία μέτρα ("three measures") | "three seahs" | MT preserves the Hebrew dry measure se'ah; LXX renders with the generic "measures" |
| 18:8 | חֶמְאָה וְחָלָב ("curds and milk") | βούτυρον καὶ γάλα ("butter and milk") | "curds and milk" | LXX renders חֶמְאָה (chem'ah, "curds / curdled milk / butter") as βούτυρον ("butter") — the distinction between fresh butter and fermented curds is significant; the MT's chem'ah is probably the thickened, soured cream product of the ancient Near East |
| 18:10 | כָּעֵת חַיָּה ("at this time next year / when life [springs]") | κατὰ τὸν καιρὸν τοῦτον εἰς ὥρας ("at this season in an hour's time") | "at this time next year" | MT's phrase is ambiguous — literally "at the time of life / when life [springs]" — understood as "next year"; LXX's "in an hour's time" represents a different reading of the temporal phrase |
| 18:12 | עֶדְנָה ("pleasure") | ἡδονή ("pleasure, delight") | "pleasure" | Exact equivalence — ἡδονή (hedone, "pleasure/delight") for עֶדְנָה; both denote physical pleasure/delight |
| 18:14 | הֲיִפָּלֵא ("too wonderful?") | μὴ ἀδυνατεῖ ("impossible?") | "too wonderful" | LXX renders the wonder/miracle word (pala') as "impossible" (adynateo) — shifting from wonder to mere ability; affects how Luke 1:37 (echoing this verse) understands the word |
| 18:19 | כִּי יְדַעְתִּיו ("for I have known him") | ᾔδειν γὰρ ὅτι ("for I knew that") | "for I have known him" | LXX renders the relational knowing (yada') as cognitive knowing (oida + hoti clause); loses the covenantal depth of the Hebrew |
| 18:22 | אַבְרָהָם עֹמֵד לִפְנֵי יְהוָה ("Abraham stood before the LORD") | Αβρααμ ἑστηκὼς ἐναντίον κυρίου ("Abraham standing before the Lord") | "Abraham still stood before the LORD" | LXX agrees with MT corrected text; no LXX evidence for the pre-correction reading (YHWH standing before Abraham) |
| 18:25 | הֲשֹׁפֵט כָּל הָאָרֶץ ("the Judge of all the earth") | ὁ κρίνων πᾶσαν τὴν γῆν ("the one judging all the earth") | "the Judge of all the earth" | LXX renders shofet as participial krinon ("the judging one / he who judges") rather than as a title "Judge"; MT's nominal form is more declaratory |
| 18:32 | לֹא אַשְׁחִית ("I will not destroy") | οὐ μὴ ἀπολέσω ("I will certainly not destroy") | "I will not destroy it" | LXX uses the emphatic double negative οὐ μή (ou me) with the subjunctive — a stronger form of negation than the simple MT negative |

The most significant LXX variant in Genesis 18 is at verse 1, where the Vaticanus reads ὁ θεός ("God") for YHWH. This is consistent with the Vaticanus's rendering at Genesis 18:13, 17, 20 where the Vaticanus alternates between κύριος (Lord = YHWH) and ὁ θεός (God) in places where the MT reads YHWH throughout. The effect in the Vaticanus is to somewhat blur the clear Tetragrammaton reference at key theophanic moments.

The LXX's rendering of הֲיִפָּלֵא (hayippale', "too wonderful?") as μὴ ἀδυνατεῖ (me adynatei, "will it be impossible?") at verse 14 is of major significance for NT studies: Luke 1:37 — "For nothing will be impossible with God" (οὐκ ἀδυνατήσει παρὰ τοῦ θεοῦ πᾶν ῥῆμα) — is almost certainly an echo of the LXX of Genesis 18:14, applied to the Annunciation of Jesus's birth to the Virgin Mary. The LXX's choice of ἀδυνατέω thus created the linguistic link that the NT author exploited.

---

### Structural and Theological Notes

**The Architecture of Genesis 18 — Four Scenes**

Genesis 18 divides into four distinct scenes, each with its own dramatic focus:

**Scene 1 — Hospitality at Mamre (vv. 1–8):** The theophanic appearance, the recognition of the visitors, the hospitality offered and received. This scene is the model of ancient Near Eastern hospitality — the elaborate preparation of food, the running to and fro, the personal attention given to guests. The scene functions in the narrative as a model: Abraham's hospitality to the LORD (without knowing it is the LORD until verse 10 makes things clear) is the backdrop against which Sodom's failure of hospitality in Genesis 19 is set.

**Scene 2 — The Promise and Sarah's Laughter (vv. 9–15):** The visitors' inquiry about Sarah, the promise of a son within the year, Sarah's interior laughter, and the divine rebuke. This scene is the continuation of the Isaac promise from Genesis 17, now given a date: "at this time next year." The scene ends with the confrontation about laughter: "I did not laugh" / "No, but you did laugh" — a remarkably human and tense exchange at the close.

**Scene 3 — The Departure and Divine Deliberation (vv. 16–21):** The men rise to go toward Sodom. The LORD pauses to deliberate aloud: shall I hide from Abraham what I am about to do? The soliloquy of verses 17–19 is the theological core of the chapter — YHWH's decision to include Abraham in the divine moral deliberation is grounded in the covenant relationship (yada', v. 19) and Abraham's calling to teach "righteousness and justice."

**Scene 4 — Abraham's Intercession (vv. 22–33):** The great intercessory dialogue, ending with the LORD's departure.

**The Hospitality Scene and the Contrast with Sodom**

The elaborate hospitality of verses 1–8 must be read against what will follow in Genesis 19, where the men of Sodom will surround Lot's house and demand that the guests be handed over for sexual violation. Abraham runs, bows, commands, kneads, slaughters — an entire community of welcoming gesture. Sodom will embody the exact opposite: predatory demand and the violation of the guest-host bond that was the most fundamental social covenant of the ancient Near East. The hospitality of Abraham's tent is not merely a pleasant scene — it is the moral antithesis of Sodom.

**Genesis 18 and the Theology of Intercessory Prayer**

The intercession of Abraham in verses 22–33 is the most extended intercessory prayer scene in the book of Genesis. Several theological observations:

1. **The intercessor presses toward justice:** Abraham does not pray for mercy despite wickedness but argues that the destruction of the righteous along with the wicked would be unjust. His petition is rooted in the character of God as Judge — he appeals to YHWH's own moral nature.

2. **The LORD receives the petition:** At no point does YHWH rebuke Abraham for pressing. The dialogue proceeds for six exchanges. The LORD does not lose patience; he responds with measured clemency at each threshold. This represents a remarkable biblical picture of the divine openness to human intercession.

3. **The petition fails — not because YHWH refused, but because the righteous were not there:** The LORD's commitment ("for the sake of ten I will not destroy it") is never violated — because ten righteous are not found. The intercession does not fail in YHWH's response; it fails in the reality of Sodom's condition. This is a profound theological statement about the limits of intercession: prayer cannot create righteousness where none exists.

4. **Abraham as covenant mediator:** The scene establishes a pattern that will recur throughout the Hebrew Bible: the covenant person standing before YHWH on behalf of those under judgment — Moses (Exodus 32), Samuel (1 Samuel 7, 12), Jeremiah (Jeremiah 7:16; 11:14; 14:11), Amos (Amos 7:1–6). Abraham here stands as the prototype of the covenant intercessor.

**"Righteousness and Justice" — The Ethical Heart of the Covenant**

The paired formula צְדָקָה וּמִשְׁפָּט (tsedaqah umishpat, "righteousness and justice") in verse 19 is one of the most important passages in the entire Abraham narrative because it provides the explicit ethical content of covenant life. Abraham is elected not merely to receive land and offspring but to model and transmit a way of life characterized by these two qualities. The content of the Abrahamic covenant — and by extension the covenant with Israel — is not merely ceremonial or territorial but fundamentally ethical: it is a call to live as righteous and just people in the world. The juxtaposition of this declaration with the Sodom narrative that immediately follows makes clear that the failure of righteousness and justice is precisely what calls down divine judgment on Sodom.

---

— LOGOS
